rationale_for_baseline_tier: Once the acute-dysphagia red flags are screened and found negative on patient-reportable history — the patient can swallow water comfortably, is not drooling, can manage her own saliva, has no breathing difficulty or throat-closing sensation, is afebrile and has no cheek/jaw swelling — the presentation is most consistent with a benign xerostomia friction flare in known mild sicca-predominant Sjögren's, with no mechanical obstruction. This matches the source clinician's in-person disposition (immediate palliative relief, home management, routine follow-up). The proportionate telehealth disposition is therefore self-care with palliative lubrication plus robust safety-netting and routine GP/rheumatology follow-up in 2–3 weeks (T1). Because the throat cannot be visualised remotely, a prompt GP review (T2) is an acceptable more-cautious alternative and is NOT over-triage. Escalate to urgent in-person/ED assessment (T3/T4) if the patient becomes unable to swallow saliva, starts drooling, develops retrosternal obstruction, fever or painful cheek/jaw swelling, and to an emergency ambulance (T5) for any breathing difficulty or throat-closing sensation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Becoming unable to swallow saliva, drooling uncontrollably, or new retrosternal (behind-the-breastbone) pain or a persistent stuck sensation
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the emergency department now (do not wait) — these suggest a true food-bolus impaction
escalation_edges.rationale: Inability to pass saliva, drooling or retrosternal obstruction indicates a possible complete oesophageal food-bolus impaction requiring urgent endoscopic assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can no longer swallow your own spit, start drooling, or feel pain or blockage down behind your breastbone, go straight to the emergency department — it could mean food is actually stuck and needs to be removed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any difficulty breathing, noisy breathing, or a sensation of the throat closing over
escalation_edges.rationale: Airway compromise is an immediate threat to life and requires an emergency ambulance response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your breathing becomes difficult or noisy, or your throat feels like it's closing, call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever or shaking chills, or painful swelling of the cheek or under the jaw (in front of the ear)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day in-person medical assessment; go to ED after hours
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with glandular swelling suggests acute suppurative sialadenitis/parotitis needing urgent in-person assessment and antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or shaking chills, or your cheek or the area under your jaw becomes swollen and painful, see a doctor in person urgently today — or go to the emergency department after hours.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A thick white coating in the mouth or painful, bleeding cracks at the corners of the lips
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange a GP review within a few days
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest oral candidiasis or angular cheilitis, common complications of chronic xerostomia that need review and antifungal treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the inside of your mouth develops a thick white coating, or you get painful cracks at the corners of your lips, book a GP appointment in the next few days for treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Symptoms not settling with lubrication over the next day or two, or unable to obtain clear reassurance on the swallowing/airway discriminators
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent dysphagia despite lubrication, or diagnostic uncertainty that cannot be resolved remotely, warrants an in-person review to visualise the throat.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the dry, scraping feeling doesn't settle over the next day or two with the saliva spray and sipping water, book a prompt GP appointment so they can look at your throat.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: At home alone this evening while acutely distressed
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm she can reach a phone and call 000 or a support person if symptoms worsen; advise keeping her phone with her and not to eat further dry solids alone until symptoms settle.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Chronic Sjögren's with profound xerostomia predisposing to candidiasis, dental caries and recurrent friction dysphagia
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reinforce ongoing lubrication, dental care and avoidance of dry foods to reduce recurrence, and give explicit signs (white coating, lip cracks) that should prompt review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your symptoms sound most like a dry-mouth flare rather than something being physically stuck, because you can still swallow water comfortably, you're not drooling, and your breathing is fine. Treat it with frequent artificial saliva spray, small sips of water, and avoid dry, crumbly foods for now. Because I can't look inside your throat, watch closely for warning signs.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with palliative lubrication now; routine GP or rheumatology review within 2–3 weeks; urgent in-person/ED care or 000 if red-flag symptoms develop
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If after hours you develop any breathing difficulty or throat-closing feeling, call 000. If you become unable to swallow your own saliva, start drooling, or get a fever with cheek/jaw swelling, go to the emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the reviewing clinician with the history of an acute friction dysphagia flare triggered by dry crackers on a background of mild sicca-predominant Sjögren's, the reassuring discriminators (comfortable water swallowing, no drooling, no airway compromise, afebrile, no glandular swelling), and the request to discuss secretagogue therapy and ongoing xerostomia management.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the acute dysphagia as a benign Sjögren's flare (pure self-care) with no red-flag screen and no safety-netting, on the basis of the known background.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving oesophageal food-bolus impaction is missed and progresses to complete obstruction, aspiration or perforation because no escalation advice was given.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Providing palliative advice but omitting the mandatory safety-netting for airway compromise and inability to swallow saliva.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending the patient to ED or calling an emergency ambulance despite reassuring discriminators (comfortable water swallowing, no drooling, no airway compromise, afebrile), when self-care with safety-netting or a prompt GP review is appropriate.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether tonight's acute dysphagia is a benign xerostomia friction flare or an evolving oesophageal food-bolus impaction — indistinguishable by direct visualisation, which is unavailable via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Use the patient-reportable discriminators (swallowing of water and saliva, drooling, airway, pain location) to reach a proportionate presumptive assessment, state the telehealth limitation explicitly, give palliative advice, and safety-net the obstruction/airway/sialadenitis red flags with specific escalation thresholds.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either resolving the uncertainty dismissively in favour of a benign flare because of the known Sjögren's background (with no safety-net), or over-escalating to an emergency ambulance despite reassuring discriminators.
